questions.question: In an investment-linked policy, the cost of insurance coverage is met by:
options: Cancelling units from the policy
A separate premium billed to the owner each month
Deduction from bonuses declared on the fund
The insurer, at no cost to the policy owner
questions.explanation: Insurance charges in an ILP are recovered by cancelling units held under the policy. B is wrong because no separate billing occurs. C imports par-fund language: ILPs declare no bonuses. D is wrong because the cover is never free; the policy owner's own units fund it.

questions.question: A policy owner holding an ILP owns:
options: The securities held within the sub-fund itself
A guaranteed minimum value payable on maturity
Units representing a share of the sub-fund
An entitlement to bonuses declared each year
questions.explanation: The owner holds units; the fund manager holds the underlying securities. A confuses units with direct ownership. B is wrong as ILP values are not guaranteed. D describes a participating policy.

questions.question: Which is NOT one of the stated purposes of an ILP?
options: Investment
Savings
Protection
Smoothing
questions.explanation: The three purposes are investment, savings and protection. Smoothing is a participating-fund mechanism and has no application to ILPs, where the owner receives actual unit performance.

questions.question: Compared with a traditional life policy of the same premium, an ILP generally provides:
options: Higher cover, because the charges applied are lower
Lower cover, as most of the premium is invested
Identical cover, since cover is fixed by regulation
Cover that is guaranteed to increase every year
questions.explanation: Protection is secondary in an ILP; the bulk of the premium purchases units, so cover per dollar is lower than a traditional policy. A reverses the position. C invents a regulatory standard. D describes no ILP feature.

questions.question: ILPs are classified in two ways. These are by:
options: Sub-fund risk rating and by currency
Frequency of premium payment and by product features
Distribution channel and by pricing basis
Age of entry and by sum assured
questions.explanation: The two classification methods are by frequency of premium payment (single or regular) and by product features (investment-focused or protection-focused). The remaining options list groupings the textbook does not use for this purpose.

questions.question: A single-premium ILP is best described as:
options: Mainly an investment product, with low cover
Mainly a protection product, with high cover
A savings product with a guaranteed maturity value
A product requiring monthly contributions for life
questions.explanation: A lump-sum ILP carries only a thin insurance layer, making it mainly an investment vehicle. B reverses it. C wrongly asserts a guarantee. D describes regular-premium payment.

questions.question: Ravi stops paying premiums on his regular-premium ILP and takes a premium holiday. During this period:
options: All deductions are suspended until premiums resume
Cover ceases at once, though units are preserved
Insurance charges continue to be deducted
The insurer credits interest to offset lost premiums
questions.explanation: Premiums pause; charges do not. Units continue to be cancelled to meet insurance charges, so the policy value falls and the policy may lapse if it becomes insufficient. A is the common misreading of the word 'holiday'. B is wrong because cover continues. D is fabricated.

questions.question: Mei Ling moves the value of her holdings from an equity sub-fund into a bond sub-fund without paying in any additional money. She has made a:
options: Top-up
Partial surrender
Premium holiday
Sub-fund switch
questions.explanation: Moving existing value between sub-funds is a switch. A top-up involves new money being paid in. A partial surrender withdraws value from the policy. A premium holiday pauses premium payment.

questions.question: Which correctly distinguishes a partial surrender from a full surrender?
options: A partial surrender ends the policy; a full surrender does not
A partial surrender keeps the policy in force; a full one ends it
Both terminate the policy, differing only in the charges applied
Neither has any effect on the insurance cover provided
questions.explanation: A partial surrender withdraws some value while the policy continues; a full surrender cashes out and ends both the policy and the cover. A reverses the two. C and D misstate the effect on the contract.

questions.question: The four factors to consider before recommending an ILP are insurance protection needs, risk profile, personal investment objective and:
options: Time horizon
Net worth
Marital status
Nationality
questions.explanation: Time horizon completes the four factors. Net worth is not a listed factor, and options citing a client's wealth as the reason an ILP suits them are distractors. Marital status and nationality do not appear among the four.

questions.question: A client with a low risk profile is one who:
options: Is willing to accept large fluctuations for higher returns
Is elderly, irrespective of their investment attitude
Seeks capital preservation and dislikes losses
Holds a portfolio concentrated wholly in equities
questions.explanation: Risk profile describes appetite for, and ability to absorb, investment loss. A low profile means wanting safety. A describes a high profile. B confuses age with attitude: an older client usually has a low risk profile, but age itself does not define it. D describes holdings rather than appetite.

questions.question: A representative recommends an ILP to a client solely on the basis that the client is wealthy and can afford the premiums. This reasoning is:
options: Sound, as affordability is the primary consideration
Sound, provided premiums stay within 10% of income
Unsound, as wealth is not a factor to be assessed
Unsound, though permitted for single-premium products
questions.explanation: Wealth is not one of the four factors. Suitability turns on protection needs, risk profile, investment objective and time horizon. A wealthy client with low risk tolerance remains unsuitable. B invents a threshold; D invents an exemption.

questions.question: Regarding the suitability of ILPs for older people, which is correct?
options: ILPs may not be sold to any client above the age of 65
ILPs are suitable regardless, as charges fall with age
ILPs are always unsuitable once a client has retired
Shorter time horizon and lower risk tolerance are the concern
questions.explanation: There is no age bar. The concern is what age typically brings: less time to recover from a fall, usually lower risk tolerance, and a rising cost of insurance. A and C invent prohibitions. B reverses the effect of age on insurance charges.

questions.question: As a policy owner ages, the effect on an ILP is that:
options: More units must be cancelled to meet the charges
Fewer units are cancelled as the fund matures over time
The sum assured reduces to hold the charges level
Charges are frozen at the rate set at inception
questions.explanation: Mortality charges rise with age, so a greater number of units must be cancelled each period to fund the same cover. B reverses this. C and D describe mechanisms that do not apply.

questions.question: Which statement about CPF monies used to purchase ILPs is correct?
options: Special Account savings may be invested in higher-risk products
Ordinary Account savings permit a wider product range
Only Special Account savings may be used to buy ILPs
CPF savings may not be used to purchase ILPs at all
questions.explanation: The Special Account sits closer to core retirement savings and is the more restricted of the two, admitting only lower-risk products; the Ordinary Account is the more flexible. A reverses the relationship, which is the most frequently set trap. C and D contradict the existence of CPFIS-OA.

questions.question: An ILP purchased with CPF savings may generally provide cover for:
options: Any benefit the insurer offers, including all riders
Hospitalisation and outpatient expenses only
Death, total permanent disability and terminal illness
Death only, with no disability benefit permitted
questions.explanation: CPF-funded ILPs are confined to death, TPD and terminal illness cover. A ignores the restriction entirely. B describes health insurance. D understates the permitted scope by excluding TPD and terminal illness.

questions.question: Which is a distinguishing feature of an ILP when compared with a participating policy?
options: Surplus is shared between owners and shareholders in set proportions
Returns are smoothed across good years and poor years alike
A minimum benefit is guaranteed whatever the fund returns
The policy owner bears the investment risk directly
questions.explanation: The defining shift is that investment risk passes to the policy owner. The other three describe participating policies: the 90:10 sharing rule, smoothing, and guaranteed benefits. None applies to an ILP.

questions.question: Siew Hoon pays an additional S$8,000 into her existing ILP to acquire further units. This is termed a:
options: Top-up
Switch
Reinstatement
Paid-up conversion
questions.explanation: Paying in additional money to buy more units is a top-up. A switch moves existing value between sub-funds without new money. Reinstatement revives a lapsed policy; paid-up conversion is a traditional-policy non-forfeiture option.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT apply to investment-linked policies?
options: Sub-fund switching
The 90:10 surplus sharing rule
Premium holidays
Riders
questions.explanation: The 90:10 rule governs the distribution of surplus in a participating fund and has no application to ILPs, which pay actual unit performance. Switching, premium holidays and riders are all ILP features.

questions.question: Which client is most likely to be suitably matched to a regular-premium ILP?
options: A retiree needing maximum cover at the lowest possible cost
A client needing the full sum in two years for a property
A client seeking a guaranteed return with no risk of loss
A working adult seeking growth who accepts fluctuation
questions.explanation: The match requires a growth objective, tolerance of fluctuation and a long horizon. A needs a term policy, which gives far more cover per dollar. B fails on time horizon. C seeks a guarantee an ILP cannot give.
